revision.comment: Created by translating the page "[[:en:Special:Redirect/revision/1150198962|Kagi Shrine]]"
revision.text:
[[File:嘉義神社附屬館所.jpg|thumb|Chiayi City Historical Relic Museum.]] '''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Chiayi City]], [[Taiwan]]
The shrine was built on 28 October 1915
The shrine was made into [[Martyrs Shrine|martyrs' shrine]] by the [[Taiwan|Republic of China]] after World War II. On 24 April 1994 the main hall was destroyed by a fire. The ruins are now '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
The main hall of Kagi Shrine was replaced by the Chiayi Tower in 1998. The tower's builders used a design that was inspired by a story told by the indigenous people about how the world was created. The tower is called the Sun-Shooting Tower and it has an observation deck.
Some parts of the shrine like the [[Chozuya|temizuya]] (purification pavilion), [[sandō]] (pathway), [[Stone lantern|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Chiayi_Water.JPG|Temizuya File:Chiayi_Lantern1.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern File:Chiayi_Korea_Dog.JPG|Komainu statues </gallery>
== Related pages ==

== Related websites ==

revision.comment: added [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] using [[WP:HC|HotCat]]
revision.text:
{{no citations|date=July 2023}} [[File:嘉義神社附屬館所.jpg|thumb|Chiayi City Historical Relic Museum.]]
'''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a [[Shinto shrine]] located in previously ''Soa-a-teng'' ({{zh|t=山仔頂|poj=Soaⁿ-á-téng}}), Kagi City, [[Tainan Prefecture]], [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese Taiwan]] (modern-day [[Chiayi Park]], [[Chiayi|Chiayi City]], [[Taiwan]]).
The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 6) but elevated to rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19). [[Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|martyrs' shrine]] by the [[Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called [[Sun-Shooting Tower]] and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese [[Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[temizuya]] (purification pavilion), [[sandō]] (pathway), [[Tōrō|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Chiayi Water.JPG|Temizuya Image:Chiayi Lantern1.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern Image:Chiayi Korea Dog.JPG|Komainu statues </gallery>
==See also== * [[Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple]] * [[Chiayi Confucian Temple]] * [[Chiayi Jen Wu Temple]] * [[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]
==External links== {{Commons category}} *{{in lang|zh}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20070220141614/http://www.fm933.com.tw/tower/index.htm 射日傳說 Legend of Sun Shooting] *{{in lang|zh}} [http://www.chiayi.gov.tw/traffic/park-5.htm 嘉義市史蹟資料館 Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum]

The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese [[Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[temizuya]] (purification pavilion), [[sandō]] (pathway), [[Tōrō|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.

The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese [[Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[temizuya]] (purification pavilion), [[sandō]] (pathway), [[Tōrō|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.

revision.text:
{{no citations|date=July 2023}} [[File:嘉義神社附屬館所.jpg|thumb|Chiayi City Historical Relic Museum.]]
'''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a [[Shinto shrine]] located in previously ''Soa-a-teng'' ({{zh|t=山仔頂|poj=Soaⁿ-á-téng}}), Kagi City, [[Tainan Prefecture]], [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese Taiwan]] (modern-day [[Chiayi Park]], [[Chiayi|Chiayi City]], [[Taiwan]]).
The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 6) but elevated to rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19). [[Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|martyrs' shrine]] by the [[Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called [[Sun-Shooting Tower]] and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese [[Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[temizuya]] (purification pavilion), [[sandō]] (pathway), [[Tōrō|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Chiayi Water.JPG|Temizuya Image:Chiayi Lantern1.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern Image:Chiayi Korea Dog.JPG|Komainu statues </gallery>
==See also== * [[Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple]] * [[Chiayi Confucian Temple]] * [[Chiayi Jen Wu Temple]] * [[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]

The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese [[Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[temizuya]] (purification pavilion), [[sandō]] (pathway), [[Tōrō|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.

'''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a [[Shinto shrine]] located in previously ''Soa-a-teng'' ({{zh|t=山仔頂|poj=Soaⁿ-á-téng}}), Kagi City, [[Tainan Prefecture]], [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese Taiwan]] (modern-day [[Chiayi Park]], [[Chiayi|Chiayi City]], [[Taiwan]]).

The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 6) but elevated to rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19). {{no citations|date=July 2023}} [[File:嘉義神社附屬館所.jpg|thumb|Chiayi City Historical Relic Museum.]]
'''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a [[Shinto shrine]] located in previously ''Soa-a-teng'' ({{zh|t=山仔頂|poj=Soaⁿ-á-téng}}), Kagi City, [[Tainan Prefecture]], [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese Taiwan]] (modern-day [[Chiayi Park]], [[Chiayi|Chiayi City]], [[Taiwan]]).
The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 6) but elevated to rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19). [[Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|martyrs' shrine]] by the [[Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called [[Sun-Shooting Tower]] and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese [[Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[temizuya]] (purification pavilion), [[sandō]] (pathway), [[Tōrō|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.

The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 6) but elevated to rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19). [[Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|martyrs' shrine]] by the [[Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called [[Sun-Shooting Tower]] and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese [[Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[temizuya]] (purification pavilion), [[sandō]] (pathway), [[Tōrō|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.

The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 6) but elevated to rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19). {{no citations|date=July 2023}} [[File:嘉義神社附屬館所.jpg|thumb|Chiayi City Historical Relic Museum.]]
'''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a [[Shinto shrine]] located in previously ''Soa-a-teng'' ({{zh|t=山仔頂|poj=Soaⁿ-á-téng}}), Kagi City, [[Tainan Prefecture]], [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese Taiwan]] (modern-day [[Chiayi Park]], [[Chiayi|Chiayi City]], [[Taiwan]]).
The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 6) but elevated to rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19). [[Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|martyrs' shrine]] by the [[Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called [[Sun-Shooting Tower]] and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese [[Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[temizuya]] (purification pavilion), [[sandō]] (pathway), [[Tōrō|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Chiayi Water.JPG|Temizuya Image:Chiayi Lantern1.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern Image:Chiayi Korea Dog.JPG|Komainu statues </gallery>
==See also== * [[Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple]] * [[Chiayi Confucian Temple]] * [[Chiayi Jen Wu Temple]] * [[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]

'''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a [[Shinto shrine]] located in previously ''Soa-a-teng'' ({{zh|t=山仔頂|poj=Soaⁿ-á-téng}}), Kagi City, [[Tainan Prefecture]], [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese Taiwan]] (modern-day [[Chiayi Park]], [[Chiayi|Chiayi City]], [[Taiwan]]).
The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 6) but elevated to rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19). [[Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|martyrs' shrine]] by the [[Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called [[Sun-Shooting Tower]] and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese [[Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[temizuya]] (purification pavilion), [[sandō]] (pathway), [[Tōrō|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Chiayi Water.JPG|Temizuya Image:Chiayi Lantern1.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern Image:Chiayi Korea Dog.JPG|Komainu statues </gallery>

revision.comment: Text replacement - "qqq" to "qq"
revision.text:
{{no citations|date=July 2023}} [[File:嘉義神社附屬館所.jpg|thumb|Chiayi City Historical Relic Museum.]]
'''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a [[Shinto shrine]] located in previously ''Soa-a-teng'' ({{zh|t=山仔頂|poj=Soaⁿ-á-téng}}), Kagi City, [[Tainan Prefecture]], [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese Taiwan]] (modern-day [[Chiayi Park]], [[Chiayi|Chiayi City]], [[Taiwan]]).
The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 6) but elevated to rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19). [[Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|martyrs' shrine]] by the [[Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called [[Sun-Shooting Tower]] and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese [[Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[temizuya]] (purification pavilion), [[sandō]] (pathway), [[Tōrō|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Chiayi Water.JPG|Temizuya Image:Chiayi Lantern1.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern Image:Chiayi Korea Dog.JPG|Komainu statues </gallery>
==See also== * [[Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple]] * [[Chiayi Confucian Temple]] * [[Chiayi Jen Wu Temple]] * [[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]

'''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a [[Shinto shrine]] located in previously ''Soa-a-teng'' ({{zh|t=山仔頂|poj=Soaⁿ-á-téng}}), Kagi City, [[Tainan Prefecture]], [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese Taiwan]] (modern-day [[Chiayi Park]], [[Chiayi|Chiayi City]], [[Taiwan]]).
The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 6) but elevated to rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19). [[Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|martyrs' shrine]] by the [[Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called [[Sun-Shooting Tower]] and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese [[Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[temizuya]] (purification pavilion), [[sandō]] (pathway), [[Tōrō|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Chiayi Water.JPG|Temizuya Image:Chiayi Lantern1.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern Image:Chiayi Korea Dog.JPG|Komainu statues </gallery>

revision.text:
{{no citations|date=July 2023}} [[File:嘉義神社附屬館所.jpg|thumb|Chiayi City Historical Relic Museum.]]
'''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a [[Shinto shrine]] located in previously ''Soa-a-teng'' ({{zh|t=山仔頂|poj=Soaⁿ-á-téng}}), Kagi City, [[Tainan Prefecture]], [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese Taiwan]] (modern-day [[Chiayi Park]], [[Chiayi|Chiayi City]], [[Taiwan]]).
The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 6) but elevated to rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19). [[Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|martyrs' shrine]] by the [[Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called [[Sun-Shooting Tower]] and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese [[Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[temizuya]] (purification pavilion), [[sandō]] (pathway), [[Tōrō|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Chiayi Water.JPG|Temizuya Image:Chiayi Lantern1.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern Image:Chiayi Korea Dog.JPG|Komainu statues </gallery>
==See also== * [[Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple]] * [[Chiayi Confucian Temple]] * [[Chiayi Jen Wu Temple]] * [[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]

{{no citations|date=July 2023}} [[File:嘉義神社附屬館所.jpg|thumb|Chiayi City Historical Relic Museum.]]
'''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a [[Shinto shrine]] located in previously ''Soa-a-teng'' ({{zh|t=山仔頂|poj=Soaⁿ-á-téng}}), Kagi City, [[Tainan Prefecture]], [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese Taiwan]] (modern-day [[Chiayi Park]], [[Chiayi|Chiayi City]], [[Taiwan]]).
The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 6) but elevated to rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19). [[Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|martyrs' shrine]] by the [[Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called [[Sun-Shooting Tower]] and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese [[Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[temizuya]] (purification pavilion), [[sandō]] (pathway), [[Tōrō|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.

revision.text:
{{no citations|date=July 2023}} [[File:嘉義神社附屬館所.jpg|thumb|Chiayi City Historical Relic Museum.]]
'''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a [[Shinto shrine]] located in previously ''Soa-a-teng'' ({{zh|t=山仔頂|poj=Soaⁿ-á-téng}}), Kagi City, [[Tainan Prefecture]], [[Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese Taiwan]] (modern-day [[Chiayi Park]], [[Chiayi|Chiayi City]], [[Taiwan]]).
The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ([[Taishō period|Taishō]] 6) but elevated to rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 19). [[Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|martyrs' shrine]] by the [[Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called [[Sun-Shooting Tower]] and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese [[Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[temizuya]] (purification pavilion), [[sandō]] (pathway), [[Tōrō|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Chiayi Water.JPG|Temizuya Image:Chiayi Lantern1.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern Image:Chiayi Korea Dog.JPG|Komainu statues </gallery>
==See also== * [[Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple]] * [[Chiayi Confucian Temple]] * [[Chiayi Jen Wu Temple]] * [[List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]

The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]] (purification pavilion), [[:en:Sandō|Sandō]] (pathway), [[:en:Stone lantern|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[:en:Komainu|Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.

revision.comment: Bot: sync infobox from Wikidata (P18,P131,P625,P112,P825,P361,P140,P149)
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kagi Shrine.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kagi City|qid=Q10924092}}|coordinates={{Coord|23.48138889|N|120.46916667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Chiayi Park|qid=Q10924084}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10924131]] Wikidata name: Kagi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10924131]] Wikidata name: Kagi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{no citations|date=July 2023}} [[File:嘉義神社附屬館所.jpg|thumb|Chiayi City Historical Relic Museum.]]
'''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in previously ''Soa-a-teng'' ({{zh|t=山仔頂|poj=Soaⁿ-á-téng}}), Kagi City, [[:en:Tainan Prefecture|Tainan Prefecture]], [[:en:Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese Taiwan]] (modern-day [[:en:Chiayi Park|Chiayi Park]], [[Chiayi|Chiayi City]], [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]]).
The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ([[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ([[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] 6) but elevated to Rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 19). [[:en:Prince Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|martyrs' shrine]] by the [[:en:Republic of China|Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[:en:Chiayi Tower|Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[:en:Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called [[:en:Sun-Shooting Tower|Sun-Shooting Tower]] and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese [[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]] (purification pavilion), [[:en:Sandō|Sandō]] (pathway), [[:en:Stone lantern|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[:en:Komainu|Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Chiayi Water.JPG|Temizuya Image:Chiayi Lantern1.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern Image:Chiayi Korea Dog.JPG|Komainu statues </gallery>
==See also== * [[:en:Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple|Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple]] * [[:en:Chiayi Confucian Temple|Chiayi Confucian Temple]] * [[:en:Chiayi Jen Wu Temple|Chiayi Jen Wu Temple]] * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]
==External links== {{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category}} *{{in lang|zh}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20070220141614/http://www.fm933.com.tw/tower/index.htm 射日傳說 Legend of Sun Shooting] *{{in lang|zh}} [http://www.chiayi.gov.tw/traffic/park-5.htm 嘉義市史蹟資料館 Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum]

The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]] (purification pavilion), [[:en:Sandō|Sandō]] (pathway), [[:en:Stone lantern|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[:en:Komainu|Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kagi Shrine]][[en:Kagi Shrine]][[ja:嘉義神社]][[simple:Kagi Shrine]][[zh:嘉義神社]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kagi Shrine.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kagi City|WD=Q10924092}}|coordinates={{Coord|23.48138889|N|120.46916667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Chiayi Park|WD=Q10924084}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10924131]] Wikidata name: Kagi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10924131]] Wikidata name: Kagi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{no citations|date=July 2023}} [[File:嘉義神社附屬館所.jpg|thumb|Chiayi City Historical Relic Museum.]]
'''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in previously ''Soa-a-teng'' ({{zh|t=山仔頂|poj=Soaⁿ-á-téng}}), Kagi City, [[:en:Tainan Prefecture|Tainan Prefecture]], [[:en:Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese Taiwan]] (modern-day [[:en:Chiayi Park|Chiayi Park]], [[Chiayi|Chiayi City]], [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]]).
The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ([[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ([[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] 6) but elevated to Rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 19). [[:en:Prince Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|martyrs' shrine]] by the [[:en:Republic of China|Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[:en:Chiayi Tower|Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[:en:Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called [[:en:Sun-Shooting Tower|Sun-Shooting Tower]] and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese [[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]] (purification pavilion), [[:en:Sandō|Sandō]] (pathway), [[:en:Stone lantern|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[:en:Komainu|Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Chiayi Water.JPG|Temizuya Image:Chiayi Lantern1.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern Image:Chiayi Korea Dog.JPG|Komainu statues </gallery>

[[:en:Prince Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|martyrs' shrine]] by the [[:en:Republic of China|Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[:en:Chiayi Tower|Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[:en:Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called [[:en:Sun-Shooting Tower|Sun-Shooting Tower]] and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese [[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]] (purification pavilion), [[:en:Sandō|Sandō]] (pathway), [[:en:Stone lantern|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[:en:Komainu|Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kagi Shrine]][[en:Kagi Shrine]][[ja:嘉義神社]][[simple:Kagi Shrine]][[zh:嘉義神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kagi Shrine]][[en:Kagi Shrine]][[ja:嘉義神社]][[simple:Kagi Shrine]][[zh:嘉義神社]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kagi Shrine.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kagi City|WD=Q10924092}}|coordinates={{Coord|23.48138889|N|120.46916667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Chiayi Park|WD=Q10924084}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10924131]] Wikidata name: Kagi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10924131]] Wikidata name: Kagi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{no citations|date=July 2023}} [[File:嘉義神社附屬館所.jpg|thumb|Chiayi City Historical Relic Museum.]]
'''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in previously ''Soa-a-teng'' ({{zh|t=山仔頂|poj=Soaⁿ-á-téng}}), Kagi City, [[:en:Tainan Prefecture|Tainan Prefecture]], [[:en:Taiwan under Japanese rule|Japanese Taiwan]] (modern-day [[:en:Chiayi Park|Chiayi Park]], [[Chiayi|Chiayi City]], [[:en:Taiwan|Taiwan]]).
The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ([[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ([[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] 6) but elevated to Rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 19). [[:en:Prince Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|martyrs' shrine]] by the [[:en:Republic of China|Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[:en:Chiayi Tower|Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[:en:Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called [[:en:Sun-Shooting Tower|Sun-Shooting Tower]] and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese [[:en:Shoin-zukuri|Shoin-zukuri]] architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the [[:en:Chōzuya|Chōzuya]] (purification pavilion), [[:en:Sandō|Sandō]] (pathway), [[:en:Stone lantern|stone tōrō lantern]], and [[:en:Komainu|Komainu]] statues amongst other things still exist today.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Chiayi Water.JPG|Temizuya Image:Chiayi Lantern1.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern Image:Chiayi Korea Dog.JPG|Komainu statues </gallery>
==See also== * [[:en:Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple|Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple]] * [[:en:Chiayi Confucian Temple|Chiayi Confucian Temple]] * [[:en:Chiayi Jen Wu Temple|Chiayi Jen Wu Temple]] * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan]]
==External links== {{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category}} *{{in lang|zh}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20070220141614/http://www.fm933.com.tw/tower/index.htm 射日傳說 Legend of Sun Shooting] *{{in lang|zh}} [http://www.chiayi.gov.tw/traffic/park-5.htm 嘉義市史蹟資料館 Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kagi Shrine]][[en:Kagi Shrine]][[ja:嘉義神社]][[simple:Kagi Shrine]][[zh:嘉義神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kagi Shrine]][[en:Kagi Shrine]][[ja:嘉義神社]][[simple:Kagi Shrine]][[zh:嘉義神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kagi Shrine]][[en:Kagi Shrine]][[ja:嘉義神社]][[simple:Kagi Shrine]][[zh:嘉義神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kagi Shrine]][[en:Kagi Shrine]][[ja:嘉義神社]][[simple:Kagi Shrine]][[zh:嘉義神社]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kagi Shrine.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kagi City|WD=Q10924092}}|coordinates={{Coord|23.48138889|N|120.46916667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Chiayi Park|WD=Q10924084}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10924131]] Wikidata name: Kagi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10924131]] Wikidata name: Kagi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{no citations|date=July 2023}} [[File:嘉義神社附屬館所.jpg|thumb|Chiayi City Historical Relic Museum.]]
'''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in previously ''Soa-a-teng'' ({{zh|t=山仔頂|poj=Soaⁿ-á-téng}}), Kagi City, {{ill|Tainan Prefecture|en|Tainan Prefecture|WD=Q697894|lt=Tainan Prefecture}}, {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese Taiwan}} (modern-day {{ill|Chiayi Park|en|Chiayi Park|WD=Q10924084|lt=Chiayi Park}}, [[Chiayi|Chiayi City]], {{ill|Taiwan|en|Taiwan|WD=Q865|lt=Taiwan}}).
The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 6) but elevated to Rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 19). [[:en:Prince Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=martyrs' shrine}} by the [[:en:Republic of China|Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[:en:Chiayi Tower|Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[:en:Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called {{ill|Sun-Shooting Tower|en|Sun-Shooting Tower|WD=Q10957191|lt=Sun-Shooting Tower}} and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese {{ill|Shoin-zukuri|en|Shoin-zukuri|WD=Q2576209|lt=Shoin-zukuri}} architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the {{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|WD=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya}} (purification pavilion), {{ill|Sandō|en|Sandō|WD=Q667783|lt=Sandō}} (pathway), {{ill|Stone lantern|en|Stone lantern|WD=Q12012294|lt=stone tōrō lantern}}, and {{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|WD=Q20155846|lt=Komainu}} statues amongst other things still exist today.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Chiayi Water.JPG|Temizuya Image:Chiayi Lantern1.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern Image:Chiayi Korea Dog.JPG|Komainu statues </gallery>
==See also== * {{ill|Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple|en|Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple|WD=Q10924086|lt=Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple}} * {{ill|Chiayi Confucian Temple|en|Chiayi Confucian Temple|WD=Q10924088|lt=Chiayi Confucian Temple}} * {{ill|Chiayi Jen Wu Temple|en|Chiayi Jen Wu Temple|WD=Q10924083|lt=Chiayi Jen Wu Temple}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|en|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|WD=Q6596888|lt=List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan}}
==External links== {{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category}} *{{in lang|zh}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20070220141614/http://www.fm933.com.tw/tower/index.htm 射日傳說 Legend of Sun Shooting] *{{in lang|zh}} [http://www.chiayi.gov.tw/traffic/park-5.htm 嘉義市史蹟資料館 Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum]

The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 6) but elevated to Rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 19). [[:en:Prince Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=martyrs' shrine}} by the [[:en:Republic of China|Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[:en:Chiayi Tower|Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[:en:Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called {{ill|Sun-Shooting Tower|en|Sun-Shooting Tower|WD=Q10957191|lt=Sun-Shooting Tower}} and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese {{ill|Shoin-zukuri|en|Shoin-zukuri|WD=Q2576209|lt=Shoin-zukuri}} architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the {{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|WD=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya}} (purification pavilion), {{ill|Sandō|en|Sandō|WD=Q667783|lt=Sandō}} (pathway), {{ill|Stone lantern|en|Stone lantern|WD=Q12012294|lt=stone tōrō lantern}}, and {{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|WD=Q20155846|lt=Komainu}} statues amongst other things still exist today.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Chiayi Water.JPG|Temizuya Image:Chiayi Lantern1.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern Image:Chiayi Korea Dog.JPG|Komainu statues </gallery>
==See also== * {{ill|Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple|en|Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple|WD=Q10924086|lt=Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple}} * {{ill|Chiayi Confucian Temple|en|Chiayi Confucian Temple|WD=Q10924088|lt=Chiayi Confucian Temple}} * {{ill|Chiayi Jen Wu Temple|en|Chiayi Jen Wu Temple|WD=Q10924083|lt=Chiayi Jen Wu Temple}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|en|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|WD=Q6596888|lt=List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kagi Shrine]][[en:Kagi Shrine]][[ja:嘉義神社]][[simple:Kagi Shrine]][[zh:嘉義神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kagi Shrine]][[en:Kagi Shrine]][[ja:嘉義神社]][[simple:Kagi Shrine]][[zh:嘉義神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kagi Shrine]][[en:Kagi Shrine]][[ja:嘉義神社]][[simple:Kagi Shrine]][[zh:嘉義神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kagi Shrine]][[en:Kagi Shrine]][[ja:嘉義神社]][[simple:Kagi Shrine]][[zh:嘉義神社]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kagi Shrine.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kagi City|WD=Q10924092}}|coordinates={{Coord|23.48138889|N|120.46916667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Chiayi Park|WD=Q10924084}}}}
{{no citations|date=July 2023}} [[File:嘉義神社附屬館所.jpg|thumb|Chiayi City Historical Relic Museum.]]
'''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in previously ''Soa-a-teng'' ({{zh|t=山仔頂|poj=Soaⁿ-á-téng}}), Kagi City, {{ill|Tainan Prefecture|en|Tainan Prefecture|WD=Q697894|lt=Tainan Prefecture}}, {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese Taiwan}} (modern-day {{ill|Chiayi Park|en|Chiayi Park|WD=Q10924084|lt=Chiayi Park}}, [[Chiayi|Chiayi City]], {{ill|Taiwan|en|Taiwan|WD=Q865|lt=Taiwan}}).
The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 6) but elevated to Rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 19). [[:en:Prince Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=martyrs' shrine}} by the [[:en:Republic of China|Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[:en:Chiayi Tower|Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[:en:Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called {{ill|Sun-Shooting Tower|en|Sun-Shooting Tower|WD=Q10957191|lt=Sun-Shooting Tower}} and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese {{ill|Shoin-zukuri|en|Shoin-zukuri|WD=Q2576209|lt=Shoin-zukuri}} architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the {{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|WD=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya}} (purification pavilion), {{ill|Sandō|en|Sandō|WD=Q667783|lt=Sandō}} (pathway), {{ill|Stone lantern|en|Stone lantern|WD=Q12012294|lt=stone tōrō lantern}}, and {{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|WD=Q20155846|lt=Komainu}} statues amongst other things still exist today.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Chiayi Water.JPG|Temizuya Image:Chiayi Lantern1.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern Image:Chiayi Korea Dog.JPG|Komainu statues </gallery>
==See also== * {{ill|Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple|en|Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple|WD=Q10924086|lt=Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple}} * {{ill|Chiayi Confucian Temple|en|Chiayi Confucian Temple|WD=Q10924088|lt=Chiayi Confucian Temple}} * {{ill|Chiayi Jen Wu Temple|en|Chiayi Jen Wu Temple|WD=Q10924083|lt=Chiayi Jen Wu Temple}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|en|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|WD=Q6596888|lt=List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan}}
==External links== {{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]]*{{in lang|zh}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20070220141614/http://www.fm933.com.tw/tower/index.htm 射日傳說 Legend of Sun Shooting] *{{in lang|zh}} [http://www.chiayi.gov.tw/traffic/park-5.htm 嘉義市史蹟資料館 Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:sn_crud " to "[[Category:sn_crud|TEST"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kagi Shrine]][[en:Kagi Shrine]][[ja:嘉義神社]][[simple:Kagi Shrine]][[zh:嘉義神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kagi Shrine]][[en:Kagi Shrine]][[ja:嘉義神社]][[simple:Kagi Shrine]][[zh:嘉義神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kagi Shrine]][[en:Kagi Shrine]][[ja:嘉義神社]][[simple:Kagi Shrine]][[zh:嘉義神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kagi Shrine]][[en:Kagi Shrine]][[ja:嘉義神社]][[simple:Kagi Shrine]][[zh:嘉義神社]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kagi Shrine.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kagi City|WD=Q10924092|1=Kagi City}}|coordinates={{Coord|23.48138889|N|120.46916667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Chiayi Park|WD=Q10924084|1=Chiayi Park}}}}
{{no citations|date=July 2023}} [[File:嘉義神社附屬館所.jpg|thumb|Chiayi City Historical Relic Museum.]]
'''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in previously ''Soa-a-teng'' ({{zh|t=山仔頂|poj=Soaⁿ-á-téng}}), Kagi City, {{ill|Tainan Prefecture|en|Tainan Prefecture|WD=Q697894|lt=Tainan Prefecture|1=Tainan Prefecture}}, {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese Taiwan|1=Taiwan under Japanese rule}} (modern-day {{ill|Chiayi Park|en|Chiayi Park|WD=Q10924084|lt=Chiayi Park|1=Chiayi Park}}, [[Chiayi|Chiayi City]], {{ill|Taiwan|en|Taiwan|WD=Q865|lt=Taiwan|1=Taiwan}}).
The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō|1=Taishō era}} 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō|1=Taishō era}} 6) but elevated to Rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 19). [[:en:Prince Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=martyrs' shrine|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} by the [[:en:Republic of China|Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[:en:Chiayi Tower|Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[:en:Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called {{ill|Sun-Shooting Tower|en|Sun-Shooting Tower|WD=Q10957191|lt=Sun-Shooting Tower|1=Sun-Shooting Tower}} and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese {{ill|Shoin-zukuri|en|Shoin-zukuri|WD=Q2576209|lt=Shoin-zukuri|1=Shoin-zukuri}} architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the {{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|WD=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}} (purification pavilion), {{ill|Sandō|en|Sandō|WD=Q667783|lt=Sandō|1=Sandō}} (pathway), {{ill|Stone lantern|en|Stone lantern|WD=Q12012294|lt=stone tōrō lantern|1=Stone lantern}}, and {{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|WD=Q20155846|lt=Komainu|1=Komainu}} statues amongst other things still exist today.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Chiayi Water.JPG|Temizuya Image:Chiayi Lantern1.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern Image:Chiayi Korea Dog.JPG|Komainu statues </gallery>
==See also== * {{ill|Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple|en|Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple|WD=Q10924086|lt=Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple|1=Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple}} * {{ill|Chiayi Confucian Temple|en|Chiayi Confucian Temple|WD=Q10924088|lt=Chiayi Confucian Temple|1=Chiayi Confucian Temple}} * {{ill|Chiayi Jen Wu Temple|en|Chiayi Jen Wu Temple|WD=Q10924083|lt=Chiayi Jen Wu Temple|1=Chiayi Jen Wu Temple}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|en|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|WD=Q6596888|lt=List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|1=List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan}}
==External links== {{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]]*{{in lang|zh}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20070220141614/http://www.fm933.com.tw/tower/index.htm 射日傳說 Legend of Sun Shooting] *{{in lang|zh}} [http://www.chiayi.gov.tw/traffic/park-5.htm 嘉義市史蹟資料館 Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum]

'''{{Nihongo|Kagi Shrine|嘉義神社|Kagi jinja|lead=yes}}''' was a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in previously ''Soa-a-teng'' ({{zh|t=山仔頂|poj=Soaⁿ-á-téng}}), Kagi City, {{ill|Tainan Prefecture|en|Tainan Prefecture|WD=Q697894|lt=Tainan Prefecture|1=Tainan Prefecture}}, {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Japanese Taiwan|1=Taiwan under Japanese rule}} (modern-day {{ill|Chiayi Park|en|Chiayi Park|WD=Q10924084|lt=Chiayi Park|1=Chiayi Park}}, [[Chiayi|Chiayi City]], {{ill|Taiwan|en|Taiwan|WD=Q865|lt=Taiwan|1=Taiwan}}).
The shrine was built on 28 October 1915 ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō|1=Taishō era}} 4) facing south but later altered in 1942 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 17) to face west. The shrine was originally categorized as a prefectural shrine in 1917 ({{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō|1=Taishō era}} 6) but elevated to Rank of {{nihongo4|small shrine|国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} in 1944 ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 19). [[:en:Prince Yoshihisa|Prince Yoshihisa]], {{nihongo4||大国魂命|Ōkunitama no Mikoto}}, [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto]], {{nihongo4||少彦名命|Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} and [[Amaterasu]] were enshrined as deities.
The ''[[honden]]'' (main hall) was turned into a {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=martyrs' shrine|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} by the [[:en:Republic of China|Republic of China]] government after World War II but was destroyed in a fire on 24 April 1994. The main office and purification hall now serve as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''.
In 1998 the [[:en:Chiayi Tower|Chiayi Tower]] was built in place of the main hall, the design was inspired by an [[:en:Taiwanese aborigines|indigenous]] mythological tale about the creator of the world. In Chinese the tower is called {{ill|Sun-Shooting Tower|en|Sun-Shooting Tower|WD=Q10957191|lt=Sun-Shooting Tower|1=Sun-Shooting Tower}} and houses an observation deck.
The existing {{nihongo4|main office|社務所|shamusho}} and {{nihongo4|purification hall|斎館|saikan}} are wooden structures built in the classical Japanese {{ill|Shoin-zukuri|en|Shoin-zukuri|WD=Q2576209|lt=Shoin-zukuri|1=Shoin-zukuri}} architectural style and underwent repair work before being opened to the general public on 5 January 2001 as the '''Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum'''. The area became part of Chiayi Park and the {{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|WD=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}} (purification pavilion), {{ill|Sandō|en|Sandō|WD=Q667783|lt=Sandō|1=Sandō}} (pathway), {{ill|Stone lantern|en|Stone lantern|WD=Q12012294|lt=stone tōrō lantern|1=Stone lantern}}, and {{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|WD=Q20155846|lt=Komainu|1=Komainu}} statues amongst other things still exist today.
==Gallery== <gallery> Image:Chiayi Water.JPG|Temizuya Image:Chiayi Lantern1.JPG|Stone tōrō lantern Image:Chiayi Korea Dog.JPG|Komainu statues </gallery>
==See also== * {{ill|Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple|en|Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple|WD=Q10924086|lt=Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple|1=Chiayi Cheng Huang Temple}} * {{ill|Chiayi Confucian Temple|en|Chiayi Confucian Temple|WD=Q10924088|lt=Chiayi Confucian Temple|1=Chiayi Confucian Temple}} * {{ill|Chiayi Jen Wu Temple|en|Chiayi Jen Wu Temple|WD=Q10924083|lt=Chiayi Jen Wu Temple|1=Chiayi Jen Wu Temple}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|en|List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|WD=Q6596888|lt=List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|1=List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan}}
==External links== {{Commons Category:100 oldest pages]]*{{in lang|zh}} [https://web.archive.org/web/20070220141614/http://www.fm933.com.tw/tower/index.htm 射日傳說 Legend of Sun Shooting] *{{in lang|zh}} [http://www.chiayi.gov.tw/traffic/park-5.htm 嘉義市史蹟資料館 Chiayi City Historical Relics Museum]
